desc:
The road here seems to disappear into the horizon, both to the east and to the
west. Quiet plains and lush forests border the road. Waymeet lies somewhere to
the west, while Hobbiton lies to the north.
name: The East Road

desc:
All around you are fields of corn, wheat, vegetables, and various other things
that the hobbits grow. Every now and then you can see a busy hobbit running from
field to field doing one of many tasks that are needed to keep these fields in
their current condition. The Border Road passes through the fields to the south.
To the east you can see a small pond.
name: Cultivated Farmland

desc:
A tilled field lies before you, obviously not yet planted. The plough still lies
in the corner of the field and everything is prepared for planting. It simply
looks as if the farmer has not had the time to plant this area of his fields.
There is a minor irrigation system laid out around the edges of the field. To
the east you can see a large barn; thick bushes block passage to the north.
name: A Quiet Field

desc:
Right in the middle of all this farmland, this little pond looks completely out
of place. However, the hobbit who farms this land seems to have made the best of
it. A rather complex irrigation system has been erected in the area, feeding
most of the crops in this field and even some of the neighbouring fields. A
small creek runs south from here. To the north you can see a large barn.
name: A Small Pond

desc:
Around you are acres of pastures in lightly rolling hills. This area seems to be
well used, as patches of grass seem to have disappeared, leaving only mud and
dirt where they used to be. However, there is still much grass left for the
livestock to feed upon. To the north you can see the centre of the farm.
name: Rolling Pastures

desc:
Lush green grass grows here, this pasture seems not to have been used at any
time in the near past. It looks as though the owners of the farm have this
pasture off on rotation, although, with one look at the field to the west, it
might be time they moved the cows back over to here. A fence separates this
field from the field to the east. A small gate leads north to the farm.
name: A Small Pasture

desc:
You are in a large barn. To the east, you can see the centre of a large dairy
farm, and all around you are cow stalls and troughs. The rafters hold much hay
for the cows, and there are bottles, casks, and barrels of milk stored neatly
against one wall. The barn itself is enormous, being approximately twenty-five
yards wide, fifty yards long, and ten yards high.
name: A Large Barn

desc:
Around you is the workings of a decent sized dairy farm. You are not too far
from the Shire's largest village, Hobbiton, and you can imagine that this dairy
farm is the major source for milk and cheese in the area. The owners of this
dairy farm must be quite prosperous, as the area seems clean and there is a
small square where workers can stop and rest. In the middle of the square there
is a small water fountain. To the south, you can see a rather large pasture.
There is also a barn to the west, and the entrance to the farm is to the east.
To the north behind a hedge you can see a path surrounded by trees.
name: A Dairy Farm

desc:
You are at the entrance to a small dairy farm just outside of Hobbiton. There
are many cart and animal tracks here, and the ground is hard packed. You can
smell manure all around you as it assaults your nostrils. To the south, there is
a small pasture. To the east is a small path leading to the fields outside of
Hobbiton.
name: Entrance to a Dairy Farm

desc:
On the sides of the path, trees have been replaced by thick vegetation. The loud
humming of insects gives evidence of a nearby bog. One may follow the path back
east towards Hobbiton or continue along west.
name: Path near the Rushock Bog

desc:
The path here is fairly moist due to its proximity to water to the north. It
continues to the west while also leading east towards the village of Hobbiton.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
This path runs parallel to the water which can be faintly heard beyond a thick
line of trees. The way leads west to Little Delving and east to Hobbiton, one of
the largest towns in all the Shire.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
All around you are the sounds of many winged insects and mosquitoes. This trail
is not used too often, making it difficult to advance through the sedges. You
can go either north or south, but it is impossible to know what lies in either
direction.
name: Trail in the Sedges

desc:
The ground is damp and boggy. Here and there you come upon pools and wide
stretches of reeds and rushes filled with the warbling of little hidden birds.
You must make your way carefully to avoid falling in and getting covered by the
mud. The bog continues east while to the south a trail is discernible through a
thick stand of sedges. One can see water to the north.
name: The Rushock Bog

desc:
The ground is damp and boggy. Here and there you come upon pools and wide
stretches of reeds and rushes filled with the warbling of little hidden birds.
You must make your way carefully to avoid falling in and getting covered by the
mud. The bog continues west while there is water to the north.
name: The Rushock Bog

desc:
The river here is murky. The water has a brownish tinge to it due to the effects
of the nearby bog. Swampy plants and flowers float on the surface of the water.
Except for the humming of the mosquitoes that live in the stagnant water, it is
silent.
name: The Water

desc:
The river here is murky. The water has a brownish tinge to it due to the effects
of the nearby bog. Swampy plants and flowers float on the surface of the water.
Except for the humming of the mosquitoes that live in the stagnant water, it is
silent.
name: The Water

desc:
The river here makes a soft bend to the south, leading to the Brandywine River
to the east. The large trees are being slowly replaced by thick vegetation, and
the water is slowly becoming more and more stagnant. Mosquitoes seem to be
everywhere.
name: The Water

desc:
The river here makes a soft bend to the north and east. Around the banks of the
river trees are becoming sparse and more insects than normal are buzzing around.
name: The Water

desc:
There is a bank to the south, where you can leave this gentle river. The flowing
becomes dimmer as you get closer to the source, somewhere to the west. To the
south-east, you see a large cabin hidden in the trees.
name: The Water

desc:
The water flows gently here, cutting through the quiet plains of the Shire. With
the soft rippling of the water and the calm wind in your face, you feel as if
you could sail all the way down the Water to the Brandywine and beyond to the
far sea.
name: The Water

desc:
The river turns gently here flowing southeasterly, carrying you in its travel
through the lands of the Shire. To the south, you think you see a large cabin
half-hidden in the trees.
name: The Water

desc:
The water flows gently here, cutting across the quiet plains of the Shire. With
the wind at your back, you feel as if you could follow this river all the way to
the Brandywine and then out to the great seas of the west.
name: The Water

desc:
The water becomes more and more stagnant as you approach its source. To the
north and south you can see the plains and fields of the Shire. Off to the
north-east you see a large hill with a road near it.
name: The Water

desc:
The river turns gently from the north back towards the east, carrying you in its
travel through the lands of the Shire. To the south, you think you see a large
cabin half-hidden in the trees.
name: The Water

desc:
On the sides of the path, the fresh scent of the large trees makes your walk
pleasant. To the north there is a big wooden cabin half-hidden in the trees.
There must be water somewhere to the north as murmurs of a river can be heard.
The path leads towards Little Delving to the west while leading to Hobbiton
eastwards.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
This path runs parallel to the Water which turns west-south here. If you follow
the road west, you will find Little Delving. At some distance to the east lies
Hobbiton, the largest village in all the Shire.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
You have now entered a large wooden cabin. It is well equipped with camping
supplies. Judging by the contents of the room, the inhabitant here must really
have a passion for nature. A door leads eastward into another room.
name: A Wooden Cabin

desc:
This room is where Valindur the Ranger teaches woodcraft to pupils willing to
learn. Many strange herbs are stored in pots and several bookshelves hold tomes
on the various arts of riding, healing, and hunting.
name: Lessons Room

desc:
Around you grows many small bushes. Gooseberries, currants and raspberries grow
here in abundance. These fields seem to be treated much differently than the
other fields in the area, as fruits do not need as much attention as vegetable
fields or corn fields do. The fields suddenly end to the north as they come to a
small path. To the west you can see a pasture behind a fence.
name: A Berry Field

desc:
Many different types of flowers grow here. Mainly golden poppies and mountain
laurels, but there are a fair amount of roses and purple lilacs to one side as
well. Some smaller plantings of various herbs and some onions also make up much
of this area.
name: A Small Flower Garden

desc:
This path follows the same general path as the water to the north. On the sides
of the path are rows of trees which provide the traveller with ample shade. To
the east lies the village of Hobbiton while to the west lies Little Delving.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
This path follows along The Water westwards. A fairly busy village can be heard
to the north-east, which could possibly have a place to rest after your long
adventures. The path runs west towards the town of Little Delving.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
This path follows the same general path as the water to the north. On the sides
of the path are rows of trees which provide the traveller with ample shade. To
the east lies the village of Hobbiton while to the west lies Little Delving.
name: Path Surrounded by Trees

desc:
Lush fields stand between the East Road, and the village of Hobbiton. Well
tended, and separated from the surrounding countryside by lush, and neatly
trimmed hedgerows, these fields obviously see frequent use, and a narrow trail
cuts north to south, where the people of the Shire use it as a short- cut from
the main road to the numerous smials, which you can see a small distance to the
north.
name: Fields near Hobbiton

desc:
This is the village of Hobbiton, one of the most important places in the Shire.
Many hobbits have their home here or in the surroundings, and the town has good
stores where the people can find what they need. To the east is a well equipped
General Store, and to the north you see the centre of the village.
name: Hobbiton Village

desc:
In this large well equipped store, one can find almost anything. On the numerous
shelves are every imaginable kind of supplies necessary for a long journey.
name: Hobson's General Store

desc:
As you enter, you are taken aback by the quality of the clothes here. The owner,
Heldor, is not here currently. He must be out scouring the surrounding lands for
the clothes he sells. A sign hangs above the entrance.
name: Heldor's Clothes Emporium

desc:
The street progresses east and west through the heart of the village. To the
south stands Heldor's Clothes Emporium, and to the north a private dwelling.
name: Small Street

desc:
You are at the eastern border of Hobbiton. To the west you can hear the sounds
of merchants selling their wares and of children playing in the streets. A cosy
little hobbit hole lies to the north. The village ends here, and the Bywater
Road begins in the east.
name: East of Hobbiton

desc:
To the west is the centre of the town. This street crosses east-west through the
village. To the north resides the ever popular Tembur's Quality Pet Store, and
more shops can be seen to the east. The centre of the town lies west.
name: Small Street

desc:
This is a modest shop of animals. Some of them are cute, just to be used as
companions, and others are tough beasts of work. Their sounds fill the room. As
you look in the cages, you see some little animals that would do real well as
pets. Will you take one with you?
name: Tembur's Quality Pet Store

desc:
Small round windows set into the front of the room offer a view of the Small
Street to the south, and are framed by neat curtains of a jolly blue fabric,
with bright yellow flowers. Like most hobbit holes, this is a neatly kept place,
cleanly swept and with new paint on all the woodwork.
name: In a Small Hobbit Hole

desc:
You are now inside a comfortable hobbit hole. Many small windows provide a good
view of the beautiful countryside while keeping the place well ventilated. A
stone fireplace against a wall is ready to provide warmth in the winter when
needed. A large pantry is visible, and, in classic hobbit tradition, very well
stocked.
name: A Comfortable Smial

desc:
Built in the middle of the largest village in the Shire, this square is often
used as a gathering place for reunions and parties. The square is very clean and
appears to be well kept by the fine inhabitants of this village.
name: Hobbiton Central

desc:
This rather wide path leaves the village of Hobbiton towards the lands north. A
bridge lies ahead, allowing you safe passage over the water. You can see a large
hill farther up the road. To the west is a small dock used to launch boats onto
the water.
name: Road to the Hill

desc:
A bridge made of solid wood allows travellers to pass over the water without
fear of getting wet. To the north, a rather large hill can be seen, while the
village of Hobbiton can be spotted to the south.
name: Bridge over the Water

desc:
The road leading to the hill is wide and its sides are covered by flowers and
soft grass. High and beautiful trees give the air purity and a sensation of
calm, making the walk easier as the road becomes steep. To the east you can see
a mill, working with the waters of the river.
name: Road to the Hill

desc:
Here the road heading north from the village of Hobbiton begins to climb the
tall green hill which overlooks all the surrounding countryside. A humble lane
leads off to the west along The Hill's lower flanks. To the east is a large
building, the door of which is partially obstructed by a pile of metal scraps.
name: Hobbiton North Place

desc:
A huge glowing fire stands in the middle of the place, overheating the whole
building. A large heap of coal is lying against the northern wall and a huge
bellows is filling the eastern part of the forge. Against the southern wall
several unfinished tools are standing: a few picks, some shovels and other
common gardening tools.
name: The Forge

desc:
This narrow lane travels along The Hill to the west. The slope of the hill above
it to the north is terraced; lush garden plots and vineyards can be seen.
name: Bagshot Row

desc:
This small and little-used lane (judging from the relative absence of ruts)
extends east and west along the southern flank of The Hill. A few modest
residential holes have been delved in the bank to the north of the road, each
with its own front garden.
name: Bagshot Row

desc:
This is the western end of Bagshot Row, a modest residential lane. Some
hobbit-homes, each with its own garden before the round door, line the northern,
hilly sides of the road. This last garden is an especially handsome, well-kept
one.
name: Bagshot Row

desc:
You are now in the garden of Bagshot Row, home of the Gamgee family. The well
maintained flowers and plants are amazing, and it is apparent that they are the
work of an artist. Sam "The Uncle" Gamgee is famous for his secrets in the
maintenance of a good garden, and looking around, it is easy to see why. A
little path of stones leads west towards a door.
name: Gamgee's Garden

desc:
This is the house of the family Gamgee. It is a nice place, though a little
modest. There are tools for gardening kept in one corner. This is not surprising
since the family has a long history as gardeners. In fact, many say that they
are the best in the whole Shire.
name: Gamgee's House

desc:
This hole is a typical low-class hobbit house. It is a poor hole, also very well
maintained and arranged. The first sight announces clearly one of the major
point of the hobbit life: food. Everything here seems to be designed to bring
the most delight for meals: large table, comfortable chairs, large kitchen, huge
cupboards, probably filled with food-stuff.
name: Twofoot's House

desc:
This garden is obviously maintained by a good gardener: it looks rather healthy
and some appetizing scents are carried on the soft wind. The rows of vegetables
are straight: one can recognise celery, garlic, onions, tomatoes, potatoes,
leeks and even cauliflower.
name: Twofoot's Garden

desc:
The road winds up The Hill and eastward around its green flank toward the
village of Overhill. In the lush sides of The Hill, to the north, can be seen
some windows and a round green door; the path leading to that door is barred by
an iron gate.
name: Road around the Hill

desc:
This wide path bends around the Hill and leads towards the small village of
Overhill. There is an animal pen directly east and the hill, itself, stands to
the west.
name: Road to Overhill

desc:
This wide path wraps around the hill towards the small village of Overhill. To
the east are large spans of green fields, and to the west is the hill itself.
This area doesn't seem to be as densely populated as the rest of the Shire.
name: Road to Overhill

desc:
Apart from the thick dust and the cobwebs this smial seems to be a dwelling
similar to all the others that can be found in the shire. Below the dust of many
years the floor is made of wood and covered with a thick carpet. A chair stands
next to a cold fireplace in the south-eastern corner while old cobwebs cover
everything of a formerly well-equipped kitchen. The round window has also been
securely boarded up and an old picture is barely visible below a thick layer of
dust next to it.
name: In a Dusty Hobbit Hole

desc:
This was formerly a goat pen, but some of the boards and rails which comprised
it have been hastily taken down to be used for another purpose. Some of the
windows have been broken out. The floor is covered with scattered hay.
name: An Abandoned Pen

desc:
You have finally reached the village. To the west a few smials are clustered
together, forming the nucleus of the village. This village seems even more
peaceful than the others in the Shire.
name: Road to Overhill

desc:
You are standing in the middle of a small group of houses that form this tiny
little village. This place is very quiet, except for the wind rustling through
the trees. The hobbit holes here do not seem to be as luxurious as those in
Hobbiton. A path winds up the Hill to the south.
name: Overhill Village

desc:
This is a large and comfortable smial, the dwelling of a well-off hobbit, or so
it seems. Comfortable furniture rests on soft carpeting; polished molding lines
the round doorways which lead off to the other sections of the home.
name: A Large Smial

desc:
You are in a charming place away from the hustle and bustle of the village.
Small trees grow on the sides of the square, inviting you to take rest beneath
their boughs. A large and beautiful smial lies to the north and to the south is
the Post Office of Hobbiton. A small street leads eastward to the centre of the
village, while to the west it turns into a placid lane heading through the
fields towards Needlehole and Little Delving.
name: White Square

desc:
This street goes east and west through Hobbiton. Here you can see small houses
on each side of the road. It does not appear to be a very busy area. To the west
the street ends at a charming square while to the east is the centre of the
village.
name: Small Street

desc:
In this building can be found the local post office of the Shire. Many hobbit
employees run around busily, organizing letters in order to get them to their
destiny. You can check the desk to see if anything has arrived for you.
name: Post Office

desc:
The flowing of the water becomes more and more stagnant as you approach its
source. Just to the east the river flows under a small bridge. To the south is a
small wooden dock where you can leave the water.
name: The Water

desc:
The water becomes more and more stagnant as you approach its source. To the
north and south you can see the plains and fields of the Shire. Off to the
north-east you see a large hill with a road near it.
name: The Water

desc:
There are many bedrooms along the hallway. Other than the hobbits, there's not
much of interest here.
name: Living Quarters

desc:
You are in a large dining hall. A long table runs the length of the room, though
it is small for anyone but a hobbit or dwarf.
name: Dining Hall

desc:
You are in a large kitchen. There are several large fireplaces, for cooking
food. From the size of things, you think, this place could probably feed a small
army. Then you remember the famous appetites of hobbits.
name: Kitchen

desc:
You have entered a musty wine cellar. Beer barrels line the north wall. Wine
bottles lie upon racks about the room. You'd better not get caught here!
name: Wine Cellar

desc:
This is the Tooks' pantry, and, as such, it is often full of good things to eat,
that family being large, energetic, and affluent. On the other hand, as they are
equally hungry, perhaps the shelves will be empty, while the cooks are busy
preparing the next meal or course.
name: The Pantry

desc:
The bank here is ideal for launching boats into the water north. To the south
lies a small path, a white picket fence blocks your way into the garden to the
east.
name: Bank of the Water

desc:
You are completely surrounded by flowers. Every possible flower you can think of
is here. Red and White roses, Lilacs, Mayflowers, Violets, Carnations,
Mistletoe, Apple Blossoms, and many more. Although, there seems to be an
abundance of Lilies of the Valley closer to the middle of the garden. Almost in
the centre of the garden, is a quaint little gazebo, painted white, with ancient
vines stretching up into its rafters. There are benches in all four corners of
the garden, and lanterns lit at intervals around the garden. To the north, you
can see the Water as it makes its way towards the Brandywine River.
name: A Beautiful Flower Garden

desc:
One can tell from the thick vegetation and the loud hum of insects on the sides
of the path that a bog is nearby. The path leads eastwards while to the north a
trail breaks in between the sedges.
name: Path near the Rushock Bog

desc:
An old birch marks the beginning of what seems to be a long line of trees
continuing as far east as eye can see. The lowest branches of the tree are too
high to reach, and the trunk is simply too big to scale without any support.
name: Beginning of a Tree Line

desc:
This hilltop is the highest point for leagues in any direction. Below you, to
the south, the little village of Hobbiton lies in the lush, tree-lined valley of
the stream called only The Water. Further south, a road can be dimly seen,
running east and west. The sides of the Hill are terraced and covered with
vines, fruit trees, and vegetable gardens.
name: On the Hill

desc:
A wagon trail comes from the north and turns east, entering a small village.
This must be how the crops from the fields in the northwest are transported to
the hobbits living in the central villages. There are a few smials among the
fields, built on the sides of small hills.
name: Near a Village

desc:
Several bushes of various size grow all over this field. The short grass and
signs in the ground suggest that this is common feeding ground for sheep or
other farm animals. The bushes grow thicker, yet not impassable, to the east.
name: Grass among Bushes

desc:
The path coming from the forest in the west continues through farmed fields and
leads toward a small hut in the east. The fields seem to be carefully planned,
even a small irrigation system has been built and connected to a nearby spring.
name: Farm Fields

desc:
These fields have been recently harvested, although there is still some live
crop growing. There are carrots, lettuce and radishes planted on both sides of a
wide but shallow ditch that runs south to the river. According to the tracks on
the ground this is the favourite place of all the local rabbit population.
name: Vegetable Fields

desc:
The fields around here are prospering due to careful farming by their owners.
Full of a variety of crops, it is obvious almost anything could be grown in this
fertile soil. A small ditch runs south through the fields where a few wooden
planks form a bridge across it. The bridge is clearly well travelled judging by
the tracks in the ground which show it to be often crossed with a carriage.
name: Prospering Fields

desc:
There are lot of sounds here that indicate presence of wildlife, but even the
sharpest eye would not catch all that hide behind the dense foliage of the
underbrush. An east-west path traverses through this small island of forest.
name: Lively Forest

desc:
Some vegetables and wild corn grow here and there, but otherwise there is no
sign of organized farming nearby. There is no obvious explanation to why the
fields have been abandoned even though the river in the south would provide all
the moisture for succesfull farming. A few ditches have been dug in the ground
long ago, but they are now overgrown as well.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
Hay grows all over this field, and much of it has already been piled below a
small shelter in the centre of the field. Tracks of a pony and a carriage form
an often used trail from the shelter, leading southeast. The field extends far
to the north, where it turns into bushes.
name: Large Fields of Hay

desc:
A lack of trees in the area has allowed taller bushes to take root. Many of them
blossom in the summer, their peace only disturbed by random travellers on the
small footpath going west and east through the opening.
name: Break in the Forest

desc:
Many small rocks have been neatly set in the riverbank to serve as stairs
leading down to the river. Above, the riverside has been found to be perfect
spot for a lonely smial, which by the look of the yard houses some very active
hobbit children.
name: A Riverbank

desc:
The forest gets darker and gloomier here, the underbrush becoming thick with
shrubs and small vegetation. The bushes create excellent hiding places for many
small animals that have left their markings along the path that runs east-west.
name: Path in the Dark Forest

desc:
The river to the south has eroded its way deep into the ground, forming a steep
bank here. The ground slopes slighly upwards in the north and as it slowly turns
into forest. Some kind of wooden rail has been built on the slope, possibly this
is used to send logs down to the river.
name: A Slope by the River

desc:
The forest grows denser to the north, while here it is pretty light and
colourful thriving in the vast amount of sunlight that reaches the ground. A
small footpath traverses up from the bog in the west and continues east through
the forest, circling around trees like a snake.
name: Path in the Forest

desc:
As the ground slopes slightly toward east it becomes drier. There are still
numerous small pools and patches of reeds in the vicinity. A couple of long
planks have been set upon the ground, making it possible to walk between dry
patches without wetting ones' toes.
name: The Bog

desc:
There is very little solid ground here, and even the few patches are covered
with reeds. The water is rather shallow however, allowing some hobbits to manage
exploring this area when the water level is low.
name: The Bog

desc:
Only a small ridge of solid ground reaches to the bog from the east, making a
way through many small pools of water. While the water flows freely in the river
south of here, in the west and north the surface is hidden by endless stretches
of reed.
name: Boggy Riverside

desc:
The soil gets more swampy as it descends into a slight bog in the east. Slowly
it becomes more difficult, yet not overly dangerous, to travel through. The
ground is quite uneven, allowing for small pools of water to collect upon the
ground.
name: Riverside Fields

desc:
The fields on this side of the river would be good for farming if not for the
fact it looks like the water seems to often flood this area. The soil is not
quite as swampy as it seems to be on the south side of the river. Still,
efficient farming would require more work against the flooding than the hobbits
probably consider worthwhile.
name: Riverside Fields

desc:
This smial doesn't look very sturdy. The walls need painting, the floor needs a
deep cleaning, and the roof is in disrepair. It appears that the room will not
offer much protection if a storm comes. The dust and cobwebs lead you to believe
the place has been abandoned.
name: A Deserted Smial

desc:
Due to excessive moisture from the nearby bog, only a few larger bushes have
been able to hold their root in the wet soil long enough to gain size. The
taller they grow, the higher becomes the risk of a strong autumn wind simply
pulling them up from the loose ground.
name: Low Shrubs

desc:
It is obvious that this smial was built with children and their play in mind.
Plenty of open space has been left in the centre of the round main hall and
everything that might be dangerous to little ones has probably been put safely
away in the closets and cabinets that line the walls. A few steps of stairs lead
outside.
name: Inside a Smial

desc:
The road bends softly here. The air here is fresh and clean, and many pretty
flowers grow by the wayside. This road is often used, as the many cart wheel
tracks and hoof prints would indicate.
name: The East Road

desc:
Around you are many acres of hay fields, however, they seem to have been
harvested some time in the recent past. The rolls of hay are piled relatively
neatly in the south-west corner of the fields. A small flower garden lies to the
west; to the north you can see a small path running behind a line of trees.
name: A Large Hay Field

desc:
The inside of the door to this home is painted a colorful grassy green with a
knob in the centre. The floor is made of rich old wood and a large window cut
out of the far wall overlooks a well-tended garden. Teacups and biscuits lie
strewn on the modest table along with what looks to be the resident's daily mail
delivery. Oftentimes one can hear the kettle steeping more tea or the chirp of
the birds outside the large window.
name: The Home of Baradoc

desc:
The old and ragged walls of this room are composed of packed dirt, some of which
falls to the ground from time to time only to be trampled by the countless
members of a hairfooted society. In one corner of the room is a mound of rubbish
that appears to have been collected for quite a period of time. While much of
the rubbish is inorganic, an odor is still emitted from the pile that would make
any elf gag. To the west and north rooms with better lighting and more appealing
content can be found.
name: Filthy Entrance

desc:
This room is scented with the wonderful makings from a hobbit's feast and the
after dinner smoke of mauve petals. Roast mutton and biscuits, libation and
mauve, the warmth of the glowing lights, all contribute to an atmosphere of
delight and perpetual relaxation. Glorious feast must be held here! Candles and
lanterns, when lit, set this room afire with the glow of a thousand fireflys.
Were it not for the window to the west, which allows some of the light to
escape, one might be blinded by the intensity of the lights. In the middle of
this room is a grand table made from the stumps of several large oak trees.
Around this table there are six wooden chairs, crafted by Tolman himself.
name: Dining Hall

desc:
This room has obviously just been dug directly out of the ground. The walls and
ceiling have been rubbed incredibly smooth. This room was built for someone of
short stature, as the ceiling is only 4 feet high. Earthen tunnels continue to
the East and West.
name: Underground Foyer

desc:
This room is made for visiting. The walls, once again, are dug out of the earth
and rubbed incredibly smooth. Several sitting areas are scattered around, where
visitors can sit and share stories of their journeys.
name: Journey Room

desc:
This room is hardly more than a depression in the dirt. Plush pillows are laid
out for sleeping. A soft glow eminates from a tiny banked fire in the corner.
The walls of smooth dirt reflect earthy colours in the light and sooth the
senses.
name: Smooth's Repose

desc:
Many carts and animals have travelled this path, as the ground here is
well-packed. To the south you can see a field that is full of small bushes. The
path runs west-east here, entering a large farm to the west and joining another
path to the east.
name: A Path to a Small Farm

desc:
This cubic house, obviously not using the genuine hobbit architecture is used by
the Little Folk of Hobbiton for storing all kind of supplies: potatoes,
tomatoes, wheat, meat, pipe-weed, and so on. All is well-ordered, classified
according to type and sorted per owner. Just next to the entrance door, leaning
against the wooden wall, a small desk can be seen, covered with papers.
name: The Old Grange

desc:
The Water flows calmly to the north here making a soft sound when it caresses
the wood of the docks. It is like a cooing song, and you could well stay for a
nap. The river can be entered here, but you need a boat to do it. Then you'll be
able to explore it and go wherever it takes you. A post in the sand here has a
small note attached.
name: Docks on the Bank of the Water

desc:
This is a scene of disorder, uncharacteristic of the Shire. In front of a
dwelling dug into the hillside to the east, flowers have been tramped into the
mud, and the grass is marked up by many footprints. A door to the east has been
nailed firmly shut, its lock stopped up with lead, and a sign has been hung on
it, written in black ink.
name: In Front of a Purple Door

desc:
Farthing Lane winds gently westward into Hobbiton, bordered by soft, rolling
hills on either side. The path is well-trodden, its surface packed smooth by
countless footsteps and cartwheels over the years. Neatly tended hedgerows frame
the lane, giving way occasionally to glimpses of distant fields and clusters of
round-doored homes nestled into the landscape.
name: Entrance to Farthing Lane

desc:
Hobbit holes dot the gentle slopes alongside Farthing Lane, their round doors
painted in a variety of earthy tones. Each home is nestled into the hillside,
framed by tidy gardens and small chimneys that rise just above the grassy
mounds. The lane passes close to these dwellings, the scent of tended earth and
blooming flowers lingering in the air.
name: Farthing Lane

desc:
Farthing Lane splits ahead, branching into two smaller paths. The northern lane
winds toward a cluster of hobbit holes nestled near the foot of a low hill,
while the southern lane curves gently toward the edge of a meadow, where a few
homes are scattered among the tall grass. Both paths are framed by hedgerows,
their leaves whispering softly in the breeze.
name: Farthing Lane

desc:
South Farthing Lane stretches gently southward, its edges lined with grassy
verges and the occasional hobbit hole nestled into the hillside. The path
meanders through quiet fields, where the soft rustling of leaves in the breeze
is the only sound. As it moves farther from the main road, the lane becomes
narrower, framed by hedgerows that whisper with each passing breeze.
name: South Farthing Lane

desc:
South Farthing Lane heads south, curving gently as it passes a few scattered
hobbit holes tucked into the rolling hills. The lane narrows slightly, flanked
by tall hedges and wildflowers, before it begins to wind westward, following the
rise of a low hill. As the path ascends, the landscape stretches out in the
distance, with patches of farmland and the occasional cluster of hobbit homes
scattered along the way.
name: Bend along South Farthing Lane

desc:
South Farthing Lane stretches west to east, winding through quiet fields and
passing by hobbit holes nestled into the hillsides. The lane follows the
contours of the land, gently sloping as it heads east, where the landscape opens
up to more open meadows and distant farmland. The path is peaceful, with the
soft murmur of wind through the grass and the occasional chirp of birds
overhead.
name: South Farthing Lane

desc:
Farthing Lane runs north to south, lined with Hobbit holes nestled into the
slopes along its edges. Round doors of green, blue, and brown peek out from
grassy hills, each surrounded by neat gardens and low stone walls. The lane
curves gently as it passes the homes, the earth packed firm beneath as it winds
toward its destination.
name: North Farthing Lane

desc:
North Farthing Lane winds up from the south, passing a series of well-tended
hobbit holes nestled along the slopes. As the lane rises gently, it curves to
the west, following the natural incline of the land. The path is bordered by low
stone walls and clusters of trees, with the distant sight of the hills to the
north becoming clearer as the lane bends.
name: North Farthing Lane

desc:
North Farthing Lane stretches west to east, its path lined with hobbit holes and
small gardens that blend into the surrounding hillside. The lane follows the
gentle rise of the land, curving slightly as it moves toward the east, where the
hills slope down toward the valley below. The quiet of the countryside is only
broken by the occasional rustle of leaves or the distant sound of a stream.
name: North Farthing Lane

desc:
Luck's hobbit hole, a cozy retreat nestled into a gentle slope, reflects its
owner's fortunate nature. A round, green door, adorned with a lucky clover,
hints at the good fortune of the owner. A tunnel-like hallway leads to circular
rooms filled with warmth and comfort. The living room, with its soft cushions
and roaring fireplace, is perfect for sharing stories and sipping tea. A
well-stocked pantry and a colourful garden just outside the window complete this
haven of peace and prosperity.
name: Luck's Hidden Smial

desc:
This is a hobbit home, much like any other hobbit home to be found in the Shire.
Rather mundane by hobbit standards, it is clean and cozy. Built into a hill near
the riverbank of the water, the sound of flowing water is everpresent here.
name: Demure Hobbit Home
